title: The Storie of Asneth
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00110, an encoding of Henry Noble MacCracken's 1910 JEGP article and edition. MacCracken transcribed the unique Middle English witness from photographs of what he called Ellesmere MS No. 4, now The Huntington Library, mssEL 26 A 13, current ff. 116r–127r (his ff. 121a–132a). His article prints the Middle English poem and an immediately following 49-line lament as an 'Epilogue,' with corresponding excerpts from Vincent of Beauvais's Speculum historiale VI.118–124 at the foot of the printed pages. The U-M encoding serializes those parallel English and Latin streams. Later scholarship identifies the English poem as a translation of a fuller Latin Liber de Aseneth / Historia Assenech recension represented by Corpus Christi College, Cambridge MSS 424 and 288; the translator's actual physical Latin exemplar remains unidentified. The lament's status as an original epilogue is disputed and is separately indexed by DIMEV.
review_notes: The intellectual subject of the paired metadata manifest is the anonymous Middle English Storie of Asneth, with the final 49-line lament retained as an associated and disputed boundary, not the whole 1910 article.
MacCracken's 'Ellesmere MS. No. 4' is the former Bridgewater Library designation of current Huntington mssEL 26 A 13; current foliation ff.116r–127r corresponds to his ff.121a–132a.

MacCracken explicitly says that he copied his text from photographs arranged with the Earl of Ellesmere's permission. The historical photographs are therefore modeled between the edition and manuscript; their present location is unknown.

The Latin Speculum historiale text printed by MacCracken is editorial comparison material, not the Middle English translator's physical exemplar and not an intrinsic second language of the cataloged medieval work.

MacCracken's claim that no copy of the fuller Historia Assenech was known to him records the state of knowledge in 1910. Peck's inspected METS discussion reports Dwyer's later identification of the surviving fuller Latin text, but none of its witnesses is asserted to be the translator's actual copy.
The complete Huntington and Parker codices are cached in ignored ZIP bundles. Work locators point into those complete objects rather than replacing them with cropped extracts.

Huntington's aggregate contributor heading includes John Walton among authors represented elsewhere in the codex. It is not component-level evidence that Walton wrote the Storie; current work-level sources call the poem anonymous.

The DIMEV core record describes a conventional 933-line extent while ending at the Storie's own explicit on f.126v, and cross-refers to a separate record for the seven-stanza lament on ff.126v–127r. That tension is preserved rather than normalized away.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00110
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00110; the file is 115,896 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00110
entities.description: A public electronic text of MacCracken's complete article selection: modern English introduction, Middle English prologue and narrative, the associated lament labeled '[Epilogue],' Vincent of Beauvais's Latin comparison, and a closing English note. The print's page-by-page English/Latin parallel is serialized as separate XML divisions.

entities.label: The Storie of Asneth
alternate_labels: As I on hilly halkes lodged me late
entities.description: An anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Joseph and Aseneth narrative, probably composed around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century. Its 32-line prologue says that a lady requested a Latin-to-English translation. The narrative is predominantly in rhyme royal and survives uniquely in Huntington mssEL 26 A 13. A 49-line personal lament follows in the same witness and is associated with the poem, but whether it was originally its epilogue remains disputed.

date_statements.label: Composition or translation
date_statements.value: Probably around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century

notes: No medieval author is named. John Walton has been proposed on circumstantial grounds, but the attribution is not accepted here as known authorship.
West Midland is retained as a dialect description, not silently converted into a proven place of composition.

entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken, The Storie of Asneth (1910)
entities.description: The first edition of the unique Middle English poem, published with an English introduction and commentary and Latin comparison excerpts from Vincent of Beauvais. MacCracken labels the final seven-stanza lament as an epilogue and describes his insertions and footnotes.

bibliographic.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version,' Journal of English and Germanic Philology 9, no. 2 (1910): 224–264.
bibliographic.title: The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version
bibliographic.container_title: Journal of English and Germanic Philology

entities.label: Historical photographs of Ellesmere MS No. 4 used by MacCracken
entities.description: A photographic surrogate arranged for MacCracken through librarian Strachan Holme with the Earl of Ellesmere's permission. MacCracken says that his Middle English text was copied from these photographs. No present holding or public reproduction was established.
entities.reproduction_format: Photographs; format and extent not established

entities.label: The Huntington Library, mssEL 26 A 13
entities.description: A composite Middle English parchment codex produced in England around 1450. The unique Storie of Asneth witness occupies current ff.116r–126v; the associated seven-stanza lament continues from f.126v to f.127r. MacCracken used the older foliation ff.121a–132a.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: This dates the physical witness, not the translation event.

holding.notes: Purchased with the Bridgewater Library in 1917; cited by Huntington as El 26 A13, Egerton family papers.

physical_description.format: Bound codex; target text in textura
notes: Bound around 1840 in purple morocco with the Bridgewater crest.
The manuscript as a whole contains Middle English and Middle French materials; this target work is Middle English.
The target begins with a large decorated initial on f.116r and ends partway down f.127r.

entities.label: Huntington complete digital facsimile of mssEL 26 A 13
entities.description: The provider's complete 277-canvas color IIIF object, from front cover through spine. The Storie and associated lament occupy canvases 245–267, labeled current ff.116r–127r.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with 277 color canvases

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of JEGP volume 9
entities.description: A complete-volume PDF and page-image object. MacCracken's article appears at printed pp.224–264, Internet Archive leaves n234–n274.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF with OCR and item metadata

entities.label: Vincent of Beauvais, Speculum historiale VI.118–124
alternate_labels: Historia Assenech excerpts in the Speculum historiale
entities.description: Vincent of Beauvais's thirteenth-century abridged Latin treatment of Joseph and Aseneth. MacCracken printed chapters VI.118–124 beneath the English text as a comparison, while recognizing that omitted material required a fuller Latin source for the English translation.

entities.label: Liber de Aseneth / Historia Assenech (fuller Latin recension)
entities.description: A fuller medieval Latin version of Joseph and Aseneth that supplies material omitted from Vincent's abridgment and is identified by later scholarship as the textual source tradition translated by the Middle English poet. Extant witnesses include CCCC MSS 424 and 288; the English translator's own exemplar is not identified with either codex.

entities.label: Joseph and Aseneth
entities.description: The earlier Greek narrative tradition about Joseph's marriage to Aseneth, her conversion, and the attack by Pharaoh's son. The fuller Latin recension is a version in this wider transmission; no surviving Greek manuscript is asserted as the direct physical source of the Middle English translation.

entities.label: Corpus Christi College, Cambridge, MS 424
entities.description: A twelfth-century Latin manuscript preserving Liber de Aseneth on ff.42r–59v. M. R. James treated it as the oldest copy known to him and collated it with CCCC MS 288.

entities.label: Parker Library complete digital facsimile of CCCC MS 424
entities.description: The complete 154-canvas color IIIF object. Liber de Aseneth occupies canvases 89–124, labeled ff.42r–59v.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest with 154 color canvases

entities.label: Corpus Christi College, Cambridge, MS 288
entities.description: A Latin miscellany associated with Christ Church, Canterbury and dated around 1200. Liber de Aseneth occupies ff.88r–97r; the cataloged component continues through f.98r with two following poems whose music was not supplied.

entities.label: Parker Library complete digital facsimile of CCCC MS 288
entities.description: The complete 264-canvas color IIIF object. Liber de Aseneth occupies canvases 183–201, labeled ff.88r–97r; canvases 202–203 continue the broader cataloged component through f.98r.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest with 264 color canvases

entities.label: Russell A. Peck, Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse (1991)
entities.description: A later Middle English Texts Series edition and study of the poem. Its online introduction reassesses date, dialect, Latin source, manuscript foliation, and the final lament, which it retains only as a tentative epilogue probably not originally part of the Storie.

bibliographic.citation: Russell A. Peck, ed., Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1991).
bibliographic.title: Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse

entities.label: DIMEV records 623 and 27
entities.description: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse indexes the Storie under IMEV 367 / NIMEV TM 195 and the following personal elegy separately under IMEV/NIMEV 2. The paired records expose the disputed epilogue boundary and identify the Huntington witness and editions.

rights.component: Modern XML markup and repository-specific contribution
rights.basis: No file-specific license for the checked-in XML was established. Public-domain status of the medieval text and 1910 edition does not automatically determine rights in modern markup.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The current U-M CME collection states that its materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright–United States.

rights.component: Medieval Middle English literary content, including the associated medieval lament
rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work and fifteenth-century witness are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, photographs, and markup are separately scoped.

rights.component: 1910 article, transcription, editorial notes, and typography
rights.basis: The article was published in the United States in 1910 and is outside the maximum U.S. copyright term; Internet Archive metadata also marks the scanned item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and item access
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata asserts NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the complete volume. No item-specific Creative Commons license was established.
The fifteenth-century content is centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership and image-provider terms remain separate.

rights.component: Huntington digital manuscript images and IIIF service
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest supplies no machine-readable open license and directs users to Huntington's rights policy. That policy says no permission is required to quote or publish public-domain material where Huntington does not own copyright, but this is not converted here into a Creative Commons license for the image files.
notes: The complete local bundle is private and Git-ignored. Confirm current terms with Huntington before redistributing its image files, especially in a commercial or print-on-demand context.

rights.component: Medieval Latin work and its ancient source tradition
rights.basis: The medieval Latin and earlier Greek literary content are centuries outside copyright term; modern editions and photographs are separate components.
The provider's IIIF manifest applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 and asks users to contact the Parker Library for higher-resolution scholarly or commercial publication.
The provider's IIIF manifest applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 and asks users to contact the Parker Library for higher-resolution scholarly or commercial publication.
The versioned source release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.

editorial_practices.description: MacCracken says that his alterations are chiefly insertions needed for the sense, that he clearly marks them, and that he gives the exact manuscript reading in footnotes.
editorial_practices.source_feature: Omitted, abbreviated, or difficult manuscript readings
editorial_practices.representation: Editorially supplied letters or text, often typographically distinguished, with manuscript readings in notes

editorial_practices.description: Many italic letter sequences in the Middle English transcription correspond to editorial expansions or supplies. The XML retains these as HI REND='italic', but not every italic segment has yet been classified against the manuscript.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English prologue, story, and lament
editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviation marks and editorially supplied readings
editorial_practices.representation: Italic type in the 1910 print, retained as XML HI elements

editorial_practices.description: MacCracken prints the following seven-stanza lament under the supplied heading '[Epilogue].' Later indexing and Peck's discussion treat its original attachment to the Storie as uncertain.
editorial_practices.source_feature: An untitled lament immediately following the Storie in the same manuscript context
editorial_practices.representation: Bracketed modern heading and continuous article lineation

editorial_practices.description: The printed Vincent comparison runs beneath the Middle English text page by page. CME00110 instead encodes the complete English section first and the Latin comparison as a later DIV1.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp.228–264 / XML English and Latin DIV1 elements
editorial_practices.source_feature: Page-by-page parallel Middle English and Latin layout
editorial_practices.representation: Serialized language streams that retain matching printed page breaks

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00110.xml.
evidence.locator: IDG; complete TEXT; preface, poem, Latin version, and commentary divisions
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for item identity, source-edition structure, headings, incipits and explicits, Middle English and Latin streams, printed page breaks, lineation, notes, and italic typography.

evidence.citation: Reproducible structural audit of CME00110.xml.
evidence.locator: Element counts and division boundaries
evidence.summary: Counts 4 DIV1, 10 DIV2, 1 DIV3, 15 HEAD, 934 L, 134 LG, 185 MILESTONE, 37 NOTE, 18 P, and 78 PB elements. The 934 L elements comprise 932 verse lines plus two rows of asterisks; the Middle English core consists of a 32-line prologue and 851-line narrative, followed by a 49-line lament. The Latin comparison contains nine prose paragraphs and the closing commentary one.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'The Storie of Asneth,' Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00110.
evidence.locator: Bibliographic header and full-text views
evidence.summary: Supports stable item identity, MacCracken attribution at the article layer, Urbana / University of Illinois / 1910 publication data, and public full-text access.
notes: The display's generic 'Author' label for MacCracken is bibliographic responsibility for the article, not medieval authorship of the poem.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection information and rights statement.
evidence.locator: Collection description and Access and Use statement
evidence.summary: Supports public collection access and U-M's public-domain / No Copyright–United States assertion for distributed materials.

evidence.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version,' Journal of English and Germanic Philology 9, no.2 (1910): 224–264.
evidence.locator: Printed pp.224–264 / Internet Archive leaves n234–n274; especially pp.225–226 editorial and manuscript statements
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for title, 1910 edition, unique Ellesmere No.4 witness, legacy foliation, use of photographs, MacCracken's insertion and footnote policy, the translation commission, his then-current lost-Latin claim, Vincent comparison, lineation, supplied epilogue label, and full English and Latin text.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive, Journal of English and Germanic Philology, volume 9, item journalofenglish09urbauoft, metadata and complete scan.
evidence.locator: Item metadata, file inventory, rights fields, and visible volume title page
evidence.summary: Supports exact PDF and OCR filenames, complete-volume access, and the provider's NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT assertion. The metadata's generic 1897 date is contradicted by the scanned source and is not used for this article.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, 'Regiment of princes: manuscript, [approximately 1450],' mssEL 26 A 13, Huntington Digital Library.
evidence.locator: Call Number, Cite As, Date, Physical Description, Notes, Provenance, and Rights
evidence.summary: Official evidence for current call number, c.1450 dating, aggregate Middle English and Middle French languages, parchment extent and dimensions, binding, Bridgewater provenance, acquisition in 1917, and Huntington rights pointer.
notes: The aggregate contributor list names authors represented across the codex and is not component-level authorship evidence for the Storie.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, IIIF manifest for mssEL 26 A 13.
evidence.locator: 277 canvases; work begins canvas c244 / f.116r and ends canvas c266 / f.127r
evidence.summary: Supports complete color facsimile access, exact current folio labels, canvas count and URLs, and the work's position within the full codex. The manifest contains no machine-readable open license and points to Huntington's policy.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of The Huntington Library mssEL 26 A 13 digital images, current ff.116r–127r.
evidence.locator: images/000245.jpg through images/000267.jpg; endpoints inspected directly
evidence.summary: Confirms the decorated initial and incipit on f.116r, the uninterrupted manuscript sequence, the Storie's ending and following lament on f.126v, and the lament's final lines partway down f.127r.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, Library Rights and Permissions.
evidence.locator: Permission, copyright, citation, and substantial-use guidance
evidence.summary: States that no permission is required to quote or publish public-domain material or material for which Huntington does not own copyright, asks users to cite the Library, places rights-holder identification on researchers, and asks those publishing a substantial portion or relying on a manuscript to inform the Library.

evidence.citation: Russell A. Peck, ed., Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse (METS, 1991), online Storie of Asneth introduction and text.
evidence.locator: Introduction: manuscript, date, dialect, source, and epilogue discussions
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship, unique Huntington witness, current and legacy foliation, approximate early-fifteenth-century / West Midland characterization, MacCracken's status as the only previous edition, identification of a fuller surviving Latin source after Dwyer, and cautious treatment of the lament as probably not originally part of the poem.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 623 (IMEV 367 / NIMEV TM 195) and 27 (IMEV/NIMEV 2).
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-623 / wit-623-1 and record-27 / wit-27-1; Manuscripts.xml HunEL26A13; Bibliography.xml MacCracken1910c and Peck1991; LICENSE
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for incipits and explicits, IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, verse forms and rhyme patterns, Huntington loci, edition references, the Storie's conventional 933-line description, and separate indexing of the seven-stanza personal elegy that MacCracken called an epilogue. The release is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, 'Asneth, Storie of,' repertory record.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, form, incipit, explicit, manuscript, editions, and studies
evidence.summary: Supports the c.1400 repertory date, conventional 933-line form, exact incipit and explicit, current and legacy Huntington foliation, MacCracken 1910 edition, and bibliography for Dwyer and Hume.

evidence.citation: R. A. Dwyer, 'Asenath of Egypt in Middle English,' Medium Ævum 39, no.2 (1970): 118–122.
evidence.locator: Article and issue record
evidence.summary: The official issue page confirms the article's author, title, journal issue, starting page, and JSTOR identifier. Its full argument was not directly inspected for this record; the statement that Dwyer identified the surviving fuller Latin text is supported here by Peck's inspected METS discussion.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, 'The Storie of Asneth: A Fifteenth-Century Commission and the Mystery of Its Epilogue,' Medium Ævum 82, no.1 (2013): 44–65.
evidence.locator: Official University of Bristol publication record and article metadata
evidence.summary: The official University of Bristol bibliographic record and abstract identify this as a peer-reviewed article, call the poem the work of an anonymous poet, and state that it critiques speculation about the commission and poet-commissioner relationship. The full article was not directly inspected for this record.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, catalog record for Corpus Christi College, Cambridge, MS 424.

evidence.summary: Official evidence for the twelfth-century Latin witness, its exact locus, rubric and decoration, and James's description of it as the oldest copy known to him and as collated with CCCC MS 288.

open_questions.question: Which physical Latin manuscript or textual subfamily supplied the anonymous Middle English translator's exemplar?
next_steps: Consult Dwyer's full textual argument and subsequent Joseph and Aseneth Latin-recension scholarship.
Collate distinctive English readings against CCCC MSS 424 and 288 and other Latin witnesses.
Keep the translation relation at abstract-work level unless a physical exemplar is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Was the 49-line personal lament originally composed as an epilogue to the Storie, attached in an exemplar, or merely copied after it?
next_steps: Consult Hume's full article and Peck's textual discussion alongside Brown and Robbins's separate treatment.
Compare hand, ruling, spacing, decoration, and textual transitions on ff.126v–127r at full available resolution.
Retain both MacCracken's epilogue association and DIMEV's separate work record in the catalog model.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous translator, exact composition date, or place be established more securely than an early-fifteenth-century West Midland linguistic association?
next_steps: Assess the proposed John Walton and patronage hypotheses against current peer-reviewed scholarship.
Distinguish translator dialect, scribal dialect, and manuscript production before assigning a composition place.
Retain MacCracken's Warwickshire suggestion as historical speculation rather than a normalized place.

open_questions.question: Do the pre-1910 manuscript photographs used by MacCracken survive, and if so where and under what access terms?
next_steps: Ask the Huntington whether photographs from the Ellesmere / Bridgewater Library survive in institutional files.
Search MacCracken archival papers and JEGP editorial correspondence.
If located, compare them with the current color facsimile to assess any lost edge or binding evidence.

open_questions.question: Can every italic span in MacCracken's Middle English transcription be assigned a precise function such as abbreviation expansion, supplied reading, or another editorial signal?
next_steps: Audit the XML HI spans against ff.116r–127r at the best Huntington resolution available.
Record segment-level categories without assuming that every italic sequence has one function.
Preserve the typography until a fully evidenced normalization is available.
